<!-- PROSE — edit freely in Obsidian -->

A radical aesthetic treatise in which Tolstoy dismantles prevailing theories of beauty and argues that true art is defined not by pleasure or technical skill but by its capacity to transmit genuine feeling from one person to another — and in so doing to unite people in shared moral consciousness. By his own criteria he condemns most of European high culture, including Shakespeare and his own earlier novels, as corrupt and elitist. A deliberately provocative work, and one of the most discussed texts in the philosophy of art.
